exemplar.title: To what extent does Vancouverâs CBD fit the core frame model?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does Vancouverâs CBD fit the core frame model?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is explicitly geographical, clearly linked to the coreâframe urban theory, and allows formulation of testable hypotheses.
Geographic context is highly specificâDowntown Vancouver, two named transects with street limits and timingâmaking primary data collection feasible.
Mapping includes two hand-annotated transects with compass rose and clear labels, showing strong locational clarity.
weaknesses: Maps lack a scale bar and formal cartographic conventions are inconsistently applied.
Hand-drawn annotations reduce professionalism and precision in spatial representation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place two elements at band 3 (question formulation and geographic context) and one at band 2 (mapping conventions). Holistically the work meets the top-band expectations for question and context, with minor mapping flawsâwarranting the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods for each hypothesis are comprehensively described and theoretically aligned (chi-square tests, Pearsonâs r, systematic transect sampling).
Justification for sampling approach and statistical tests shows good understanding of relevance to the research question.
weaknesses: Data collection timing and weather inconsistencies reduce reliability of some pedestrian counts.
Statistical notation and decision-rule language contain minor errors (e.g. ÏÂ² formula notation, sign convention for Pearsonâs r).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: One strand achieves band 3 for method description; the other sits at band 2 because of reliability issues in implementation. On balance the investigation is carefully designed but not without execution flawsâwarranting 2 marks.

strengths: Primary data (retail points, pedestrian counts, housing locations) are directly relevant to all hypotheses.
Appropriate presentation techniques (scatter plots, bar overlays, contingency tables) facilitate interpretation.
weaknesses: Graphics are occasionally hand-annotated, inconsistent in typography, and lack uniform legends or scale bars.
More advanced presentation methods (proportional symbols, GIS layers) are absent; some data gaps remain (e.g. vertical land-use).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While data relevance is strong (upper end of band 3â4) and presentation methods are appropriate (band 3), recurring convention lapses and missing advanced techniques limit the work to the lower half of the 3â4 rangeâwarranting 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Statistical techniques (chi-square, Pearsonâs r) are applied correctly, with calculation steps shown.
Data interpretation links trends and anomalies to the coreâframe model and research question throughout.
Analysis covers all three hypotheses with discussion of theoretical implications and anomaly explanation.
weaknesses: Evidence gaps remain (e.g. vertical land-use, time-controlled pedestrian counts) leading to incomplete coverage.
No formal assessment of regression assumptions (residual/Q-Q plots) and confidence levels are omitted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands fall solidly in the 5â6 band (analytical techniques and data linkage), with the third at the upper 3â4 band due to minor gapsâyielding a holistic score of 5 marks for strong but not fully comprehensive analysis.

strengths: Conclusion clearly revisits each hypothesis and ties findings back to the coreâframe model.
All statements are fully supported by statistical results and mapped evidence.
weaknesses: Does not quantify effect sizes or confidence intervals to deepen analytical rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is succinct, explicit, and fully supported by the analysisâmeeting the descriptor for band 2 (clear, fully supported conclusion).

strengths: Evaluation identifies key limitations (vertical land-use, timing variation, weather) and explains their impact on data reliability.
Improvement suggestions are relevant and feasible (synchronous counts, additional data collection).
weaknesses: Depth of impact analysis is superficial; lacks quantification of bias magnitude or direction.
Improvement protocols are recommended but not detailed (e.g. control thresholds for weather).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands place both evaluation depth and improvement suggestions at band 2. The student outlines appropriate strengths/weaknesses and relevant improvements but without detailed impact analysisâwarranting 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two location maps are supplied, containing a compass rose and labelled transects; however, they lack a scale bar and use hand-drawn annotations that depart from formal cartographic conventions. Detail is therefore either excessive or informal, matching the descriptor for band 2 rather than the highest band.

The fieldwork question is clearly phrased, explicitly geographical (CBD morphology) and rooted in a recognized urban theory (core-frame model). The wording easily permits the derivation of three testable hypotheses, demonstrating clear theoretical linkage and relevance to the IB syllabus. Hence the quality aligns with the top-band descriptor.

Maps, scatter plots, bar overlays and contingency tables are all appropriate and aid interpretation. While effective, some graphics are cluttered or hand-annotated, and more advanced representations (e.g., proportional symbols, GIS layers) are absent, indicating solid but not âmost effectiveâ usageâband 3 of this range.

Techniques (transect surveys, timed pedestrian counts) are appropriate and mostly executed correctly, yielding sizeable data sets. Nevertheless, acknowledged timing and weather inconsistencies reduce reliability, so the evidence corresponds to âcorrectly used but with minimal shortcomingsâ, i.e. band 2.

The student applies chi-square tests and Pearsonâs correlation correctly, displays calculation steps, and plots a regression line. Confidence levels are not calculated and some terminology ("r greater than -0.5") is imprecise, so the analysis is strong but not fully comprehensive, fitting the 5-6 band at 5 marks.

Trends, anomalies and theoretical implications are discussed for each hypothesis, with frequent reference to the core-frame model. While linkage is clear, depth could be richer regarding causality and competing explanations. The quality aligns with solid 5-6 band performance, here awarded 5 marks.

The student identifies Downtown Vancouver and narrows the study to two specific transects with exact street limits and timing of data collection. The information provided makes primary data collection completely feasible and unambiguous, satisfying the descriptor for band 3.

The evaluation notes three pertinent limitations (vertical land-use, timing variation, weather) and briefly explains their influence on reliability, satisfying the descriptor of âappropriate outlineâ rather than the exhaustive explanation expected for band 3.

A substantial body of primary data (156 retail points, 16 pedestrian sites, 20 residences) is directly relevant to all three hypotheses, allowing meaningful statistical testing. Minor gaps remain (no vertical land-use), yet overall sufficiency places the work in the upper part of the 3-4 band, warranting 4 marks.

The conclusion succinctly revisits each hypothesis and ties findings back to the core-frame model, explicitly supported by statistical results and mapped evidence. The clarity and explicit linkage meet the requirements for a fully supported conclusion, i.e. band 2.

All three hypotheses are analysed and statistically tested, yet some datasets (vertical land-use, time-controlled pedestrian counts) remain untreated, leaving noticeable evidence gaps. This corresponds to âanalysis with some gapsâ, the upper end of the 3-4 band, so 4 marks are appropriate.

Methods for each hypothesis are described in depth, including sampling approaches, variables, and chosen statistical tests (chi-square, Pearsonâs r). Each method is explicitly justified in relation to the research question and urban theory, showing comprehensive explanation and theoretical alignment.

Most figures are titled and sourced; a compass is provided, yet scale bars and uniform legend design are missing. A few tables span pages without clear labelling. These occasional convention lapses match the descriptor âoccasional errorsâ, meriting 3 marks within the 3-4 band.

For each limitation, the student proposes a realistic improvement (collect vertical data, synchronise counts, avoid rainy days) but only briefly describes expected impact. This demonstrates relevant suggestions but lacks detailed impact analysis, fitting band 2.

annotations.comment: Hypotheses are well formulated and explicitly linked to the core-frame model, allowing clear null and alternative formulations for statistical testing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Methodology description clearly justifies transect selection and timing, but weather and timing inconsistencies reduce reliability. Note need for synchronous counts to improve comparability.
Interpretation mentions CF Pacific Centre but omits it from mapped data. Incorporate major high-order retail complexes to avoid spatial under-representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Scatter plot analysis identifies outliers but lacks a residual or Q-Q plot to assess linear regression assumptions. Including these would strengthen the statistical rigor.
Conclusion reiterates hypothesis outcomes effectively but would benefit from quantifying effect sizes and confidence levels to demonstrate analytical depth.
The fieldwork question is clear, explicitly geographical, and linked to the core-frame urban theory, enabling formulation of testable hypotheses. Excellent alignment with IB syllabus requirements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographic context is thorough, situating Vancouverâs CBD on a peninsula with clear physical boundaries and rationale for transect feasibility. Strong locational specificity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 lacks a scale bar and north arrow; hand-drawn annotations are informal. Use formal cartographic conventions to improve mapping clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Mapping conventions are inconsistent: compass appears but there is no scale for Figure 2. Adding both scale and legend will meet band 3 requirements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The systematic chi-square approach for retail type is well described, but the formula uses â(0âE)Â²/Eâ instead of generic â(OâE)Â²/Eâ. Recommend using variable notation $$O,E$$ for clarity.
Decision rule text advises âaccept hypothesisâ when ÏÂ² > critical; instead, explicitly state rejection of null $$H_0$$ for correct hypothesis testing language.
Pearsonâs r threshold is mis-described: a strong negative correlation requires $$r< -0.5$$, not simply âgreater than â0.5â. Clarify the sign convention.
Hypothesis 3 method uses chi-square on presence/absence data, but low counts in some cells may violate the expected frequency assumption. Consider Fisherâs exact test or regrouping categories.
Figures 6 & 7 lack legend differentiation for low- vs high-order retail; add consistent symbology and legend for clearer interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Chi-square contingency table uses percentage bands; interval definitions are unclear. Specify exact distance ranges for reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The write-up notes visual representation âdoes not directly supportâ Hypothesis 1 despite statistical support. Ensure interpretation aligns consistently with data.
Pearsonâs r is correctly calculated but thresholds for strength should be sourced by discipline (urban studies), not Scribbr. Cite a geography-appropriate reference.
Residential distribution method cross-checks via BC Housing but omits criteria (e.g., building height thresholds). Include methodology details for replicability.
Chi-square for housing has cell counts of 1 and 5, violating expected frequency rules. Recommend combining adjacent bands or using Fisherâs exact to validate results.
Evaluation correctly outlines key limitations (vertical land use, timing, weather) but lacks reflection on impact magnitude; include discussion of bias direction and scale.
Weather improvement suggestion is practical but should specify a control threshold (e.g., wind/rain gauge) and protocol to schedule consistent fieldwork conditions.
The transect sampling approach is well justified with random systematic positioning, yielding a robust dataset for pedestrian analysis.
Some figures use inconsistent font sizes and hand labels; ensure uniform typography and digital annotation for professionalism.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Citation list includes Wikipedia; evaluate source reliability and consider peer-reviewed urban geography journals to strengthen source quality.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
